# CS 6650 Final Project
# Real-Time Distributed Monitoring System
### Kafka Analytics Pipeline · Grafana Observability · Claude Code AI DevOps Agent

**Team:** Zongwang Wang · Dylan Pan · Lucas Chen · Ellis Guo  
**Course:** CS 6650 — Building Scalable Distributed Systems  
**Date:** April 2026

---

## 1. Project Overview

This project implements a **production-grade observability platform** that attaches to any instrumented distributed service. The system collects per-request telemetry through a Kafka streaming pipeline, aggregates metrics in a 60-second rolling window, evaluates per-instance alert thresholds, visualizes the full service state in Grafana, and provides an AI-powered DevOps advisor embedded directly in the dashboard.

The **demo target** is an Album Store REST API — a photo management service deployed across 4 EC2 nodes. The album store generates realistic, diverse traffic (metadata reads, photo uploads, concurrent creates) that exercises every dimension of the monitoring system.

```
Album Store Nodes (4× EC2 c5n.large)
  │  MetricEvent per HTTP request  (Kafka, async, zero latency overhead)
  ▼
Kafka broker  ──┬── analytics consumer  →  60s aggregation  →  album-analytics-output
 (KRaft)        └── alert consumer      →  30s/instance     →  SNS email (4 recipients)
                
Prometheus  ←── scrapes /metrics (per node), redis_exporter, postgres_exporter,
                yace (CloudWatch), s3_exporter, analytics service, alert service

Grafana  ←── Prometheus  (30+ panels across 7 rows)
  └── DevOps Chat iframe  →  live metrics + Claude Code prompt generator

Claude Code Monitor (local):  poll → detect → invoke claude CLI → analyze → act
```

---

## 2. Design Choices

### 2.1  Kafka for Per-Request Event Streaming

Prometheus scraping (15s interval) misses sub-second events. A request completing in 2ms is invisible to a scrape. Kafka captures every event, enabling:
- **Exact p99** from raw samples — no estimation or approximation
- **Per-instance 30-second sliding windows** for the alert service
- **Trend detection** across consecutive 60-second analytics windows
- **Decoupling** — the monitored service emits events without knowing who consumes them

The producer uses `Async=true` with a non-blocking channel send. If the buffer is full, a goroutine is spawned. **Result: zero impact on HTTP response latency.**

### 2.2  Dual Telemetry — Kafka + Prometheus

Both channels serve distinct purposes that cannot be collapsed into one:

| Channel | Granularity | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| Kafka events | Per HTTP request | Alert thresholds, exact percentiles, trend windows |
| Prometheus `/metrics` | 15s scrape | Grafana panels, counters, gauges |
| CloudWatch (YACE) | 60s–24h | EC2 CPU, ALB latency, RDS, ElastiCache |
| S3 Exporter (custom) | 60s real-time | Object count, bucket size (bypasses CloudWatch 24h delay) |

### 2.3  Per-Instance Alert Evaluation (Not Aggregated)

The alert service maintains an independent `instanceWindow` per `instance_id`. When one of four nodes degrades, the aggregate average hides it — but the alert fires immediately on the affected node.

Alert types and conditions:

| Alert | Condition | Window | Cooldown |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIGH_LATENCY | p99 > 500ms | 30s per instance | 5 min |
| HIGH_ERROR_RATE | 5xx rate > 5% | 30s per instance | 5 min |
| LOW_CACHE_HIT | Redis hit rate < 50% | 30s per instance | 5 min |
| HIGH_CPU | EC2 CPU > 10% | CloudWatch 60s | 5 min |

Firing condition: `fireAlert()` is called only when **all samples** in the window exceed the threshold, preventing transient spikes from triggering.

### 2.4  Per-Operation Cache Instrumentation

A global cache hit rate of "95%" doesn't distinguish:
- Album metadata GETs served from Redis (fast, correct)
- Photo status polls expiring by 1-hour TTL (expected misses)

The instrumented server tracks `{op="album_get"}` and `{op="photo_get"}` separately, enabling the Grafana panel to show which operation causes DB load. This required adding Prometheus counters to the Redis `GetAlbum()` and `GetPhotoStatus()` functions.

### 2.5  S3 Real-Time Exporter (Custom)

CloudWatch publishes `BucketSizeBytes` and `NumberOfObjects` once per 24 hours — useless for a dashboard showing live upload progress. The custom `s3-exporter` container calls `list_objects_v2` every 60 seconds and exposes `s3_bucket_objects_total` and `s3_bucket_size_bytes` as Prometheus metrics. S3 list API calls cost $0.005/1000 — negligible.

### 2.6  Claude Code as the AI Agent

The original `final_project/services/monitor` shells out to:
```go
exec.Command("claude", "--print", "--dangerously-skip-permissions", prompt)
```

This project follows the same pattern. Claude Code IS the agent — it has full project context, can run AWS CLI and Terraform commands, and operates with human oversight. No API key, no separate container, no autonomous action without visibility.

Two modes:
- **Interactive (DevOps Chat)**: Grafana iframe shows live metrics, generates a formatted prompt → user copies into local Claude Code session
- **Autonomous**: `./run-monitor.sh` polls Prometheus every 30s, invokes Claude Code on alert detection

---

## 3. Experiments

### Experiment 1 — Kafka Pipeline Throughput

**Goal:** Verify the Kafka pipeline can ingest production-like traffic rates without adding backpressure to the monitored service.

**Setup:** 60-second load tests at 10, 30, 60, and 100 concurrent users. Both event ingestion rate and HTTP response latency measured simultaneously.

| Load | Events/s into Kafka | p50 (ms) | p95 (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 users | 32.3 | 14 | 190 |
| 30 users | 94.2 | 16 | 340 |
| 60 users | 163.9 | 18 | 670 |
| 100 users | 218.7 | 19 | 730 |

**Finding:** Median latency is flat at 14–19ms across all load levels — the Kafka producer adds zero observable overhead. p95 increases at higher loads due to S3 upload bandwidth contention (expected), not the monitoring system. At 218 events/s, the pipeline is well within single-broker Kafka capacity.

![Fig 1 — Kafka Pipeline Throughput](data:image/png;base64,)

---

### Experiment 2 — Alert Detection Latency

**Goal:** Measure how quickly the alert service detects a threshold breach and fires a per-instance notification.

**Setup:** Establish baseline (0 alerts). Apply burst of 30 users running large photo uploads, forcing sustained p99 > 500ms across all nodes. Monitor alert service logs for timestamps.

| Instance | p99 at Alert Fire | Alert Fired? |
|---|---|---|
| ip-172-31-27-225 | 539ms | ✅ |
| ip-172-31-25-193 | 880ms | ✅ |
| ip-172-31-21-34 | 967ms | ✅ |
| ip-172-31-30-52 | 704ms | ✅ |

**Detection timeline:**
- Load start → 0s
- First events reach alert service via Kafka → <1s
- 30s window fills with threshold-exceeding samples → 30–35s
- Alert fired, SNS email delivered → ~40s

**Finding:** All 4 nodes fired independently within their individual 30-second windows. Per-instance evaluation is confirmed: node 3 (p99=967ms) fired independently of node 1 (p99=539ms), even though both were exposed to the same workload. The window-based design prevents single-request spikes from triggering false alarms.

![Fig 2 — Per-Instance Alert Detection](data:image/png;base64,)

---

### Experiment 3 — Cache Observability Accuracy

**Goal:** Verify that per-operation cache instrumentation correctly captures Redis behavior and that Prometheus accurately reflects the difference between warm-cache and cold-cache conditions.

**Setup:** Phase 1 (warm cache): 30 users making repeated GET requests to known album IDs — cache should be hot. Phase 2 (cold simulation): 30 users requesting fresh UUID album IDs not in cache — forces misses.

| Operation | Warm Hit Rate | Cold Hit Rate | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| album_get | 97.2% | 80.1% | −17.1pp |
| photo_get | 97.3% | 85.4% | −11.9pp |

**Finding:** The monitoring system correctly distinguishes per-operation cache behavior. During the warm phase, 97%+ of GETs are Redis hits with no DB round-trip. The cold simulation forces misses that fall through to PostgreSQL — visible in `album_store_cache_misses_total{op="album_get"}`. The per-operation label is essential: a global "95% hit rate" during the cold simulation (due to mixing warm photo_get hits with cold album_get misses) would have hidden the problem.

![Fig 3 — Cache Observability Accuracy](data:image/png;base64,)

---

### Experiment 4 — End-to-End Monitoring Data Flow

**Goal:** Characterize the latency budget at each stage of the monitoring pipeline from HTTP request to SNS email delivery.

**Analysis of measured and theoretical latencies:**

| Stage | Latency | Source |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP request handling | 14–19ms (p50) | Locust measurements |
| Kafka publish (async) | <0.02ms | Code analysis (non-blocking) |
| Kafka broker buffer | ~50ms | Kafka BatchTimeout=10ms + overhead |
| Analytics consumer read | ~5ms | kafka-go read loop |
| Analytics 60s window | 60,000ms | By design (aggregation window) |
| Alert 30s window | 30,000ms | By design (detection window) |
| SNS email delivery | ~5,000ms | AWS SES typical |

**Finding:** The total end-to-end latency from event to email is bounded primarily by the alert detection window (30s by design). The Kafka producer overhead is negligible (<0.02ms). This is a deliberate design trade-off: a shorter window (10s) would detect conditions faster but produce more false positives from transient spikes.

![Fig 4 — Data Flow Timing](data:image/png;base64,)

---

### Experiment 5 — Prometheus Observability Coverage

**Goal:** Verify that all monitoring data sources are active and contributing distinct, non-overlapping metrics.

**Method:** Query `/api/v1/targets` during live load. Enumerate distinct metric families per source.

| Source | Targets | ~Metrics | Update Freq |
|---|---|---|---|
| Album Store /metrics (×4) | 4 | 8 each | 15s |
| redis_exporter | 1 | 15 | 15s |
| postgres_exporter | 1 | 25 | 60s |
| YACE CloudWatch | 1 | 35 | 60s |
| s3_exporter (custom) | 1 | 2 | 60s |
| analytics service | 1 | 6 | 15s |
| alert service | 1 | 4 | 15s |
| **Total** | **11/11 ✅** | **~100** | — |

**Finding:** Full coverage with zero redundancy between sources. Each data source answers a different question: Kafka events → exact latency distribution; Prometheus /metrics → per-operation rates; CloudWatch → EC2/ALB infrastructure; S3 exporter → storage growth in real time. The dashboard's 30+ panels map directly onto these distinct data sources.

![Fig 5 — Prometheus Coverage](data:image/png;base64,)

---

### Experiment 6 — Alert Threshold Correctness

**Goal:** Validate the alert service fires on true positives and suppresses false positives.

**Setup:** Two runs: low load (5 users, expected p99 < threshold) and high load (40 users with large uploads, expected p99 >> threshold). Count alerts after each.

| Scenario | Observed p99 | Threshold | Alerts Fired | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low load (5 users, 60s) | 650ms | 500ms | **0** | ✅ No false alarm |
| High load (40 users, 90s) | 3,900ms | 500ms | **4** (1 per node) | ✅ True positives |

**Finding:** Zero false positives. At low load, even though p99=650ms exceeds the 500ms threshold, the **30-second window** doesn't fill with sustained violations — transient spikes don't trigger. The `fireAlert()` condition requires all samples in the window to exceed the threshold. At high load, all 4 nodes sustain high p99 across the full 30-second evaluation window, firing independently.

![Fig 6 — Alert Evaluation](data:image/png;base64,)

---

## 4. Results Summary

| Metric | Result |
|---|---|
| Kafka ingestion rate (100 users) | 218.7 events/s |
| Kafka producer overhead | <0.02ms per request |
| Alert detection window | 30s (by design) |
| End-to-end: event → email | ~40 seconds |
| Per-instance alerts fired | 4/4 nodes, independently |
| False positive alerts | **0** |
| Prometheus targets active | 11/11 |
| Total metrics exposed | ~100 distinct |
| Cache observability accuracy | Per-operation (album_get, photo_get) |
| Experiment requests total | ~100,000+ across all runs |
| Experiment failure rate | **0.00%** |

---

## 5. Observability Gaps This System Closes

**Gap 1 — Hidden single-node degradation**  
Standard aggregate metrics hide one bad node behind three healthy ones. Per-instance evaluation fires the moment any single node sustains a violation, independent of the fleet average.

**Gap 2 — S3 storage visibility**  
AWS CloudWatch publishes S3 object count and size daily. The custom `s3_exporter` makes storage growth visible within 60 seconds of each photo upload — essential for a photo management service.

**Gap 3 — Cache miss granularity**  
A global cache hit rate conflates album metadata misses (expensive DB reads) with photo status TTL expirations (expected). Per-operation counters reveal which cache layer is under-performing.

---

## 6. One-Command Deployment

The full stack — 4 album store nodes, monitoring platform, Kafka, all exporters — provisions from scratch in ~15 minutes:

```bash
cd cs6650_final/album-store-monitor/
./deploy-all.sh
```

Teardown:
```bash
cd final_mastery/terraform/
aws s3 rm s3://$(terraform output -raw s3_bucket) --recursive
terraform destroy -auto-approve
aws ec2 terminate-instances --instance-ids <monitor-instance-id>
```

---

```json
    "latency_p99_ms": 500,
    "error_rate_pct": 5,
    "cache_hit_min_pct": 50
  }
}
```

## Appendix C — Comparison with Original Final Project

| Dimension | `final_project` (cart-api) | This Project (album-store) |
|---|---|---|
| Service type | ECS Fargate | EC2 c5n.large |
| Primary alert signal | CPU utilization | Request latency |
| Analytics window | 60s | 60s |
| Scale target | ECS desired_count [1,8] | EC2 node_count [1,4] |
| AI agent | Anthropic API autonomous | Claude Code CLI (interactive) |
| Metric persistence | DynamoDB | Prometheus TSDB (7d) |
| Dashboard panels | Basic | 30+ across 7 rows |
| Deploy | `docker compose up` | `./deploy-all.sh` (Terraform + SSM) |

The core producer → Kafka → consumer group → alert/aggregate pipeline is identical. The album store adaptation demonstrates the architecture's generality.
